perturbation.original_text: Watchit shall have the exclusive right to sell third party advertising as sponsors of their content and will have the right to brand the content under the Watchit brand and place a "bug" on the screen identifying the content with a Watchit trademark.
perturbation.changed_text: Watchit shall have the exclusive right to sell third party advertising as sponsors of their content; however, all advertisements must be pre-approved by Oceanic Time Warner Cable and must not be of competing services. Watchit will have the right to brand the content under the Watchit brand and place a "bug" on the screen identifying the content with a Watchit trademark.
perturbation.explanation: By introducing the requirement for Oceanic Time Warner Cable's pre-approval of advertisements and the exclusion of competing services, the 'exclusive right' is effectively limited. This creates ambiguity and potential conflict, especially if Oceanic Time Warner Cable unreasonably withholds approval. The term 'exclusive right' generally means without external limitations, except where specifically outlined. The inserted condition undermines this fundamental aspect.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text could potentially violate Section 1 of the Sherman Act, which prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. While this agreement itself might not be a direct violation, the clause giving Oceanic Time Warner Cable the right to pre-approve advertisements and exclude competing services could be used to stifle competition unfairly, especially if Oceanic Time Warner Cable has its own competing advertising services. This introduces an element of potential anti-competitive behavior not present in the original text.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a condition that Oceanic Time Warner Cable must pre-approve advertisements and that these advertisements must not be of competing services. This condition directly limits Watchit's 'exclusive right' to sell third-party advertising. The scraped law snippet, 15 U.S. Code § 1, states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' By allowing Oceanic Time Warner Cable to pre-approve advertisements and exclude competing services, the changed text introduces a potential restraint of trade, giving Oceanic Time Warner Cable control over advertising and creating an unfair advantage, contradicting the original contract's allocation of rights.

perturbation.original_text: Ocean Time Warner Cable will provide Watchit with data on viewership to both Channel 777 and specifically to the content provided by Watchit.
perturbation.changed_text: Ocean Time Warner Cable may, at its sole discretion, provide Watchit with data on viewership to both Channel 777 and specifically to the content provided by Watchit, provided such data does not violate subscriber privacy regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will provide' to 'may, at its sole discretion, provide' transforms a mandatory obligation into a discretionary one. While the addition of 'provided such data does not violate subscriber privacy regulations' attempts to justify the change, it introduces uncertainty and potentially allows Oceanic Time Warner Cable to withhold data without clear justification. The original text implies a guarantee of data provision, which is now removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 47 U.S.C. § 551 addresses cable operator's privacy obligations, the added clause gives Oceanic Time Warner Cable broad discretion to withhold viewership data under the guise of privacy concerns. This could undermine the intended purpose of providing Watchit with data to assess content performance and advertising effectiveness, essentially nullifying the original agreement by allowing discretionary non-compliance. The legal ambiguity increases the risk of dispute.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 47 U.S. Code § 551 - Protection of subscriber privacy | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 47 U.S. Code § 551 - Protection of subscriber privacy U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Notice to subscriber regarding personally identifiable information; definitions (1) At the time of entering into an agreement to provide any cable service or other service to a subscriber and at least once a year thereafter, a cable operator shall provide notice in the form of a separate, written statement to such subscriber which clearly and conspicuously informs the subscriber of— (A) the nature of personally identifiable information collected or to be collected with respect to the subscriber and the nature of the use of such information; (B) the nature, frequency, and purpose of any disclosure which may be made of such information, including an identification of the types of persons to whom the disclosure may be made; (C) the period during which such information will be maintained by the cable operator ; (D) the times and place at which the subscriber may have access to such information in accordance with subsection (d); and (E) the limitations provided by this section with respect to the collection and disclosure of information by a cable operator and the right of the subscriber under subsections (f) and (h) to enforce such limitations. In the case of subscribers who have entered into such an agreement before the effective date of this section, such notice shall be provided within 180 days of such date and at least once a year thereafter. (2) For purposes of this section, other than subsection (h)— (A) the term “personally identifiable information” does not include any record of aggregate data which does not identify particular persons ; (B) the term “ other service ” includes any wire or radio communications service provided using any of the facilities of a cable operator that
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that Ocean Time Warner Cable 'will provide' Watchit with viewership data. The changed text alters this obligation to 'may, at its sole discretion, provide,' effectively removing the guarantee of data provision. This creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the initial agreement. While 47 U.S.C. § 551, as indicated in the scraped snippets, focuses on subscriber privacy, the law explanation highlights that the added clause, although seemingly related to privacy, grants Oceanic Time Warner Cable broad discretion that can be used to avoid providing the data altogether. The snippets don't directly address mandatory data provision, but the Law Explanation argues, and the Perturbation Explanation agrees, that the discretionary change could nullify the original agreement's purpose.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Oceanic Time Warner Cable removes the above content and or terminates this agreement prior to December 31, 2006, Oceanic Time Warner Cable agrees to pay a cancellation fee of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000) per month multiplied by the number of months remain on the agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Oceanic Time Warner Cable removes the above content and or terminates this agreement prior to December 31, 2006, Oceanic Time Warner Cable agrees to pay a cancellation fee of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000) per month multiplied by the number of months remain on the agreement, *unless* such removal or termination is due to 'unforeseen circumstances'.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the caveat '*unless* such removal or termination is due to 'unforeseen circumstances'' introduces a significant ambiguity. The term 'unforeseen circumstances' is vague and can be interpreted differently, potentially nullifying the cancellation fee provision. This creates a loophole that Oceanic Time Warner Cable could exploit, making the enforcement of the cancellation fee uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Hawaii Revised Statutes § 490:2-615 deals with excuse by failure of presupposed conditions in contracts. By adding the 'unforeseen circumstances' clause without clear definition, the contract opens itself to arguments based on this statute, allowing for the potential discharge of Oceanic Time Warner Cable's obligation to pay the cancellation fee if circumstances arise that were arguably unforeseen. This weakens the original contractual obligation and introduces legal uncertainty regarding the enforceability of the cancellation fee.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces the term 'unforeseen circumstances' which introduces ambiguity, potentially nullifying the cancellation fee provision. The Law Explanation states that Hawaii Revised Statutes § 490:2-615 deals with excuse by failure of presupposed conditions in contracts and that adding the 'unforeseen circumstances' clause without clear definition opens the contract to arguments based on this statute, potentially discharging Oceanic Time Warner Cable's obligation to pay the cancellation fee. Since the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable and the modified text weakens a core legal obligation, the contradiction is explicit and impactful.
